title: Malignant Hyperthermia
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Anesthesiology, Emergency Medicine, Critical Care
objectives: Recognize early signs of MH during anesthesia

vignette.text:
A 22-year-old man undergoes appendectomy under general anesthesia with sevoflurane and succinylcholine. Thirty minutes into surgery, the anesthesiologist notices increasing ETCO2, tachycardia, and the patient is becoming rigid despite adequate anesthesia.

Past Medical History: None known

Family History: Uncle died during surgery 'from a reaction'

physicalExam.exam: Masseter muscle rigidity, diffuse muscle rigidity, mottled skin

questions.stem: This patient has hypercarbia, tachycardia, rigidity, and rising temperature during anesthesia.
questions.question: What are the clinical features of malignant hyperthermia?

options.text: MH only occurs immediately after induction

MH CLINICAL FEATURES: EARLY SIGNS: Unexplained tachycardia; Rising ETCO2 (earliest and most sensitive); Masseter muscle rigidity (after succinylcholine); Tachypnea (if spontaneous breathing); LATER SIGNS: Hyperthermia (may rise 1-2°C every 5 min); Generalized muscle rigidity; Mottled/cyanotic skin; Arrhythmias; LABORATORY: Hyperkalemia (rhabdomyolysis); Respiratory and metabolic acidosis; Elevated CK (markedly elevated); Myoglobinuria; DIC (late); TIMING: Can occur at any point during anesthesia; May occur in recovery room; May develop hours post-op; FAMILY HISTORY: Autosomal dominant; May have history of unexplained anesthetic deaths
Fever is the earliest sign
Normal ETCO2 rules out MH

questions.explanation: MH Recognition: EARLY signs - unexplained tachycardia, rising ETCO2 (earliest/most sensitive), masseter rigidity after succinylcholine. LATER - hyperthermia (can rise 1-2°C/5 min), generalized rigidity, arrhythmias. LABS: Hyperkalemia, acidosis, very elevated CK, myoglobinuria. Can occur anytime during anesthesia or even in recovery. Family history of anesthetic deaths suggests autosomal dominant inheritance.

sources.text: StatPearls: Malignant Hyperthermia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient received sevoflurane and succinylcholine.
questions.question: What triggers malignant hyperthermia?

options.text: MH TRIGGERS: VOLATILE ANESTHETICS (all): Sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane; Halothane (historic, most potent trigger); SUCCINYLCHOLINE: Depolarizing neuromuscular blocker; Can trigger alone or with volatiles; GENETIC BASIS: RYR1 gene mutation (most common); Ryanodine receptor dysfunction; Uncontrolled calcium release from SR; Autosomal dominant inheritance; SAFE AGENTS: Nitrous oxide; Propofol, etomidate, ketamine; Opioids; Benzodiazepines; Non-depolarizing NMB (rocuronium, vecuronium); Local anesthetics; NOT TRIGGERS (commonly confused): Stress, heat, exercise (in MH-susceptible); These trigger similar conditions but not true MH
Local anesthetics trigger MH
Propofol triggers MH
Only halothane causes MH

questions.explanation: MH Triggers: ALL volatile anesthetics (sevoflurane, isoflurane, desflurane, halothane) and SUCCINYLCHOLINE. Genetic basis: RYR1 mutations causing uncontrolled calcium release, autosomal dominant. SAFE agents: Nitrous oxide, propofol, ketamine, opioids, benzos, non-depolarizing NMB, local anesthetics. Heat/exercise don't trigger true MH (but can trigger related myopathies).

sources.text: StatPearls: Malignant Hyperthermia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the emergency treatment for MH?

options.text: Continue anesthesia and observe

MH EMERGENCY TREATMENT: IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: (1) STOP all triggers (turn off volatile, stop succinylcholine); (2) CALL FOR HELP - get MH cart/dantrolene; (3) Hyperventilate with 100% O2 (high flows 10+ L/min); (4) DANTROLENE: 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus; Repeat every 5 min until symptoms resolve; May need 10+ mg/kg total; Continue 1 mg/kg q4-6h × 24-48h; (5) COOL PATIENT: Iced saline IV, ice packs, cooling blanket; Stop cooling at 38°C (avoid overshoot); (6) TREAT HYPERKALEMIA: Calcium, insulin/glucose, bicarbonate; (7) TREAT ARRHYTHMIAS: Avoid calcium channel blockers (interact with dantrolene); (8) Monitor UOP (myoglobinuria risk); (9) ICU admission mandatory
Calcium channel blockers for arrhythmias
Dantrolene can wait until confirmed

questions.explanation: MH Emergency Treatment: STOP triggers immediately. DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, repeat q5min until resolution (may need 10+ mg/kg). Hyperventilate with 100% O2 at high flows. COOL patient (ice, cold saline). Treat hyperkalemia. AVOID calcium channel blockers (interact with dantrolene causing cardiovascular collapse). Mandatory ICU admission. Continue dantrolene 1 mg/kg q4-6h × 24-48h.

sources.text: StatPearls: Malignant Hyperthermia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What do you need to know about dantrolene?

options.text: Dantrolene is easy and quick to prepare
Maximum dose is 2.5 mg/kg

DANTROLENE: MECHANISM: Direct-acting muscle relaxant; Blocks calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum; DOSING: Initial: 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus; Repeat q5 min until symptoms resolve; Maximum in crisis: No absolute max - give until it works; Typical total: 10 mg/kg but may need more; Maintenance: 1 mg/kg q4-6h × 24-48h; PREPARATION: Reconstitute with sterile water (time-consuming); Each vial = 20mg (needs 60mL water); Newer formulation (Ryanodex) easier to mix; SIDE EFFECTS: Muscle weakness; Phlebitis; Hepatotoxicity (chronic use); CRITICAL: Do NOT give with calcium channel blockers (cardiovascular collapse)
Safe to combine with verapamil

questions.explanation: Dantrolene: Blocks calcium release from SR. DOSING: 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, repeat q5min until resolution (no maximum in crisis - may need 10+ mg/kg). Maintenance 1 mg/kg q4-6h × 24-48h. PREPARATION is time-consuming (20mg/vial needs 60mL water), newer Ryanodex faster. CRITICAL: Do NOT combine with calcium channel blockers (causes cardiovascular collapse).

sources.text: StatPearls: Malignant Hyperthermia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the complete management?

options.text: Single dose dantrolene is adequate

Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE: Rising ETCO2 + tachycardia + rigidity + fever + family history = MH; (2) STOP ALL TRIGGERS immediately (sevoflurane off); (3) CALL FOR HELP - activate MH protocol; (4) HYPERVENTILATE 100% O2 high flow; (5) DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg IV, repeat q5min; (6) COOL aggressively (stop at 38°C); (7) TREAT HYPERKALEMIA (calcium, insulin/glucose, bicarb); (8) AVOID CALCIUM CHANNEL BLOCKERS; (9) Monitor for: Arrhythmias, myoglobinuria/AKI, DIC, recurrence; (10) ICU admission × 24-48h minimum; (11) Continue dantrolene 1 mg/kg q4-6h; (12) LATER: Genetic counseling, screen family, MH susceptibility testing for future procedures
Complete surgery then treat MH
Outpatient follow-up is fine

questions.explanation: Complete MH Management: Recognize (ETCO2, tachycardia, rigidity, fever). STOP triggers, call for help, hyperventilate 100% O2. DANTROLENE 2.5 mg/kg repeat q5min until resolution. Cool aggressively (stop at 38°C). Treat hyperkalemia. AVOID CCBs. ICU × 24-48h with continued dantrolene. Monitor for complications. Later: genetic counseling, family screening, future anesthetic precautions.

sources.text: StatPearls: Malignant Hyperthermia. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected malignant hyperthermia?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in malignant hyperthermia. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of malignant hyperthermia. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing malignant hyperthermia, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.

options.text: Perform emergent pericardiocentesis
options.explanation: While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.

sections.title: Clinical Approach
sections.content: Systematic approach to Malignant Hyperthermia including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content: Evidence-based clinical pearls for Malignant Hyperthermia.
